1: hûʾ habᵉḵôr — Reuben was the firstborn (bᵉḵôr), yet the birthright (bᵉḵōrāh) was not his to keep. The indictment is precise: bᵉḥalᵉlô yᵉṣûʿê ʾāḇîw — “when he defiled his father's couch/bed.” ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal) means to profane, desecrate, or defile — the same root as ḥōl (common/profane) as opposed to qādôš (holy). The act (Gen 35:22, sleeping with Bilhah) is not described but its legal consequence is: the bᵉḵōrāh was transferred to Joseph's sons.
2: The Chronicler articulates a distinction between two aspects of the firstborn's inheritance: haśśᵉrārāh — the ruler/prince role — went to Judah (nagîd in some translations; here the term śar = prince/chief), while the bᵉḵōrāh (birthright = double inheritance share) went to Joseph. This two-track resolution explains the parallel importance of Judah (the royal tribe) and Joseph (the most numerous tribe) in the later history: neither invalidates the other because they received different dimensions of the firstborn's rights.
3: The standard list of Reuben's four sons matches Gen 46:9 and Exod 6:14 closely. Ḥanôḵ, Pallûʾ, Ḥeṣrôn, wᵉKarmî — the genealogical formula resumes after the theological aside of vv1-2. The bᵉnê Rᵉʾûḇên bᵉḵōr Yiśrāʾēl opening restates Reuben's formal position even as the narrative has explained why that position carries no privilege here.
4: The chain from Joel continues through eight generations (bᵉnô X — his son X, seven times). The chain uses the possessive suffix construction (bᵉnô = his son) to mark direct father-son descent, distinguishing it from the lateral bᵉnê (sons of) lists. Joel's line is the one that reaches Beerah who went into exile (v6), suggesting Joel's family was the most prominent Reubenite clan in the Assyrian period.
5: The chain continues: bᵉnô Mîḵāh bᵉnô Rᵉʾāyāh bᵉnô Baʿal. The name Baʿal in an Israelite genealogy is not unusual in the pre-monarchic and early monarchic period (cf. Eshbaal/Ishbosheth; Merib-baal/Mephibosheth) — baʿal simply meant “lord/master” and was applied to YHWH before the prophets specifically condemned its identification with the Canaanite deity.
6: ûḇᵉʾērāh bᵉnô ʾăšer hᵉglāh Tilgat-pilneser melek ʾAššûr — Beerah son of Baal, whom Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria deported. The name Tilgat-pilneser (Tiglatpileser) is a Hebraized form of the Akkadian Tukulti-apil-Ešarra (my trust is in the son of Esharra), reflecting the Assyrian throne name. The Chronicler specifies he was nāśîʾ laRᵉʾûḇēnî — a leader/prince of the Reubenites — explaining why his deportation is historically significant; removing the chief destabilized the whole tribe.
7: wᵉʾeḥāyw lᵉmišpᵉḥōtāyw — his kinsmen/relatives, listed by clan. The phrase bᵉhityaḥaśśām lᵉtôlᵉdôtām — “enrolled by genealogy in their generations” — specifies the administrative process: official genealogical registration (hitpael of yāḥas, to enroll by genealogy). Yᵉʾîʾēl hārōʾš wᵉZᵉḵaryāhû — Jeiel the chief (head) and Zechariah, the two senior Reubenite leaders.
8: wûḇelaʿ ben-ʿAzaz ben-Šema ben-Yôʾēl — Bela with a three-generation patronymic, connecting him to the same Joel line as Beerah (v6). He “settled in Aroer as far as Nebo and Baal-meon” — Aroer was on the Arnon river (Deut 2:36), Nebo was the mountain from which Moses viewed Canaan (Deut 34:1), and Baal-meon was a Moabite town (Num 32:38). Bela's clan held the core Reubenite territory east of the Jordan.
9: wĕlimdᵉmāʿ nāšāʾ midbārāh — “he settled eastward to the edge of the desert”, reaching as far as the Euphrates (hanNāhār = the River, a Hebrew idiom for the Euphrates). kî miknᵉhem rāḇû bᵉʾereṣ Gilʿād — the reason given is the growth of their livestock requiring expanded territory. The Chronicler frames territorial expansion as a natural consequence of blessing (population/livestock growth), not as military aggression.
10: ûḇimê Šāʾûl — in the days of Saul: a chronological anchor for the first Reubenite military engagement, the war against the Hagrites (Hagrîʾîm). The Hagrites were a pastoral people of the Syro-Arabian steppe, possibly connected to Hagar/Hagarites (Ps 83:6). wayyipᵉlû bᵉyāḏām — “were defeated” (lit. fell by their hands) — using the standard military defeat idiom. wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉʾohalêhem — they settled in their tents/encampments, occupying the Hagrite territory.
11: ûḇᵉnê Gāḏ lᵉnegeḏ šāḵᵉnû — the sons of Gad lived “opposite them” (lit. “facing them”), in Bashan. Bāšān was the fertile northern plateau east of the Sea of Galilee, famous for its cattle (Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12). The Gadites held the southern portion of Transjordan while the Reubenites held the central and northern zones. ʿad Śalkāh — to Salecah, the easternmost limit of Bashan (Deut 3:10), marking the extent of Gadite settlement.
12: The Gadite leadership roster: Yôʾēl hārōʾš wĕŠāpām hamišneh — Joel the chief and Shapham the second-in-command. The term hamišneh (the second, the deputy) is an administrative title used throughout Chronicles for the second-ranking officer in a hierarchy (cf. 2 Chr 28:7 for the deputy king). This shows the Chronicler working from administrative rosters, not just genealogical records.
13: Seven Gadite clan members listed — “their relatives by family” (ûʾăḥêhem lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtêhem). The number seven is complete and is perhaps intentional as a formulaic grouping. Miḵāʾēl, Mᵉšullām, Šeḇaʿ, Yôray, Yāḵān, Zîʿāʾ, wᵉʿEḇer šiḇʿāh — seven in all, as the text confirms.
14: ʾēlleh bᵉnê ʾĂḇîḥayil ben-Ḥûrî — these are sons of Abihail son of Huri, then traced through six generations: Huri, Jaroah, Gilead, Michael, Jeshishai, Jahdo, and Buz. The depth of genealogy (six ancestral links) establishes the family's antiquity and prominence in the Gadite tribal structure, comparable to the deep Simeonite pedigrees in ch4.
15: Aḥî ben-ʿAḇdîʾēl ben-Gûnî rōʾš lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtām — Ahi son of Abdiel son of Guni, head of their father's house. The three-generation patronymic for Ahi establishes him as the senior Gadite clan head. rōʾš lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtām (head of the father's house) is the standard administrative term for the leading patriarch of an extended family unit (bêt ʾāḇ).
16: wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉGilʿād bᵉḇāšān ûḇibnōtêhā — they lived in Gilead, in Bashan and its towns (bᵉnôt = daughter-towns, i.e., dependent villages). ûḇᵉkol-migrᵉšê Šārôn ʿal-tôṣᵉʾōtām — and throughout the pasturelands of Sharon to their borders. The Sharon mentioned here is likely not the coastal Sharon plain but a Transjordanian region of the same name (cf. the plural mentions in 2 Chr 4:16 of LXX; Isa 33:9).
17: kullām hitya_ḥasᵉsû bîmê Yôtām melek-Yᵉhûdāh ûḇîmê Yārāḇᵉʿām melek-Yiśrāʾēl — all were enrolled by genealogy during the reigns of Jotham king of Judah and Jeroboam king of Israel. This provides a double synchronization: Jotham (southern) and Jeroboam II (northern), both reigning approximately 782–740 BCE. The Chronicler dates his genealogical source to this period — a time of relative prosperity when administrative census-taking was feasible.
18: bᵉnê-Rᵉʾûḇên wᵉGāḏ waḥăṣî šēḇeṭ Mᵉnašše — the three Transjordanian tribes act as a military coalition. ʾanšê ḥayil (men of valor/strength) — the standard Chronicler term for capable warriors. The description of military capability is detailed: they carry shield and sword, draw the bow, and are trained in war (lāmᵉdê milḥāmāh). The census-like enumeration (“forty-four thousand seven hundred and sixty, ready for service”) reflects the administrative precision of a military muster roll.
19: wayyilaḥămû ʿim-haHagrîʾîm wᵉYᵉṭûr wᵉNāpîš wᵉNôḏāḇ — the coalition fought against the Hagrites, Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. Jetur and Naphish are sons of Ishmael (Gen 25:15; 1 Chr 1:31), identifying the enemies as Ishmaelite desert tribes. Nodab is otherwise unknown. The alliance of Ishmaelite and Hagrite (possibly descendants of Hagar) groups represents the primary pastoral-nomadic competition for the Transjordanian steppe.
20: wayyēʿāzᵉrû ʿălêhem — “they were helped against them” — the niphal of ʿāzar (to help), implying divine assistance. The reason is explicit: kî ḥāʿăqû ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm bammilḥāmāh — “for they cried out to God in the battle.” wayyēʿāter lāhem — “and he granted their plea” — the niphal of ʿātar (to pray/entreat), meaning to be entreated, to respond to prayer. The causal chain is theological: prayer → divine response → victory.
21: The spoil is enumerated in detail: 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, 2,000 donkeys, 100,000 persons. The numbers follow the convention of large-scale military victory accounts in Chronicles, where enumeration signals completeness of triumph. The comparison with similar totals in tribal conquest narratives (Num 31:32-35) shows the Chronicler drawing on a consistent literary pattern for describing total victory.
22: kî mēʾĕlōhîm hammilḥāmāh — “for the war was from God” — a theological attribution formula distinctive to the Chronicler. In parallel passages in Samuel-Kings, divine causality is present but often less explicit; Chronicles surfaces it as the primary explanation. wayyēšᵉḇû taḥtêhem — “they settled in their territory” — occupying the Hagrite steppe lands until the exile (haggôlāh), which ended the Transjordanian settlements.
23: ûḇᵉnê ḥăṣî šēḇeṭ Mᵉnaššeh — the half-tribe of Manasseh, the third Transjordanian tribe. mippārān ʿad Baʿal-ḥermôn wĕŠᵉnîr wĕhar Ḥermôn — from Bashan northward to Baal-hermon, Senir, and Mount Hermon. Hermon (also called Senir by the Amorites, Deut 3:9) was the northern limit of territory taken from Og of Bashan, establishing the extent of Manassite control.
24: Seven Manassite clan heads listed as ʾanšê ḥayil ʾanšê šēmōt — “men of valor, men of renown” — a double honor title that echoes the description of David's mighty men (ch11). The use of the same heroic vocabulary links the Transjordanian chiefs to the idealized warrior tradition of the Davidic era.
25: wayyimʿᵃlû ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇōtêhem — “they were unfaithful to the God of their fathers” — the verb māʿal (to act unfaithfully/treacherously) is the Chronicler's key term for covenant violation that brings divine judgment (used extensively throughout Chronicles; cf. 2 Chr 26:16; 28:19, 22; 29:6; 36:14). wayyiznû — “and prostituted themselves” — zānāh (to commit sexual immorality) in the metaphorical sense of covenant apostasy, following Hosea's influential use of the marriage metaphor for YHWH-Israel relations (Hos 1:2; 4:12).
26: wayyāʿer ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-rûaḥ Pûl melek-ʾAššûr — “the God of Israel stirred up (ʿûr hiphil) the spirit (rûaḥ) of Pul king of Assyria.” The verb ʿûr (to rouse, stir, incite) is used throughout Chronicles for divine initiative — YHWH rouses kings and peoples to accomplish his purposes (2 Chr 21:16; 36:22-23). Pul is Tiglath-pileser III's throne name used in Babylonian records (2 Kgs 15:19). The identification hûʾ Tilgat-pilneser (that is, Tiglath-pileser) is the Chronicler's own gloss explaining the two names for the same king. wayyiglēm — “he exiled them” — the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh were deported to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river Gozan, the standard Assyrian exile destinations (also named in 2 Kgs 17:6 for the northern exile).
